title: Copperplate uppercase: a stroke-by-stroke study
subtitle: The oval, loop, and flourish constructions that make up the Copperplate capital alphabet
excerpt: Copperplate uppercase letters are elaborate constructions built from ovals, loops, and hairline entry strokes. Each capital begins with a hairline swing, carries one or more compound curves, and typically ends with a descending loop or a horizontal finishing hairline.
sourceNotes: Platt Rogers Spencer, Spencerian Key to Practical Penmanship (1866), Part II on capitals. Internet Archive digitised copies available through HathiTrust. The Spencerian upper-case alphabet is the canonical source for the Copperplate capital proportions and stroke order described here.

glossaryTerms.definition: The fundamental form underlying most Copperplate letters. A compressed circle drawn with maximum shade (pressure) at the left curve and a hairline at the right.
The thick downstroke produced by applying pressure to the flexible nib, which causes the tines to spread and lay down a wider ink line.
A continuous stroke that changes direction mid-path without lifting the pen, transitioning smoothly from one curve into the opposing curve.

content.text: The Copperplate lower-case alphabet rewards consistent oval repetition. The uppercase is different: each capital has individual character, and the stroke count and complexity vary significantly between letters. A Copperplate C is built in one or two strokes; a Copperplate M or N requires careful planning of loops and entry curves before the pen touches the paper. The
is still the foundation, but the
and the hairline loop do more work here than in the lower case.

Copperplate capitals slope at 54 to 55 degrees from the baseline. Capital height in the Spencerian system is roughly 6 to 7 times the x-height. The x-height is set by the nib width at maximum pressure: for a Nikko G on standard practice paper, this is around 2 mm. So a capital should measure roughly 12 to 14 mm. The
in a capital is the same maximum pressure as in the lower case; the hairlines are drawn with no pressure at all.
Group 1: oval-based capitals (C, E, A, G, O, Q)

These capitals begin with an anticlockwise oval drawn with full shade on the left curve and a hairline on the right. Capital C is the prototype: one oval stroke ending in a hairline exit top-right. Capital O is two ovals joined at the top and bottom. Capital G adds a hairline extension turning right at the base of the oval. Capital A adds a loop above the oval and a hairline crossbar.
Group 2: loop-based capitals (B, D, F, I, J, P, T)

These begin with a tall ascending loop. The loop of a Copperplate B or P starts above cap height, curves left, descends as a hairline, then turns right to pick up the downstroke. The descending stem then carries the lower oval (B, P) or continues to the baseline (I, J, F). Capital I is the simplest loop letter: one ascending loop, one shaded downstroke, one descending loop.
Group 3: compound-curve capitals (H, K, M, N, R, U, V, W, X, Y, Z)

These letters use compound curves or diagonal strokes alongside the standard oval and loop. Capital M, for example, has an ascending loop on the left stem, a compound curve body that dips below the baseline, and a shorter upright right stem. Practise these in the sequence M before N before H, as each simplifies on the previous.

items.symptom: The capital letters look inconsistent in slope
items.cause: The oblique pen holder is angled differently between letters.
items.fix: Mark the 55-degree slope line on the practice sheet with a ruler and use it as a reference for every downstroke. The holder should point to the same shoulder angle consistently.

items.symptom: The loops on B, P, and F are too large relative to the oval
items.cause: The loop is drawn before considering its proportion to the letter body.
items.fix: Draw the oval first as a guide mark, then draw the loop to scale. The loop height should be roughly twice the cap height, but the loop width should be no wider than the oval.
